title: Neuro-Symbolic JSON: Bridging Structured Data and Typst Generation for NLP Venues
abstract: The formatting of natural language processing (NLP) research papers has traditionally relied on complex LaTeX templates. In this paper, we propose a novel, intermediate JSON-based schema that maps cleanly to Typst, a modern typesetting engine. We demonstrate that structuring documents in this machine-readable format allows for seamless programmatic generation of ACL-compliant PDFs without sacrificing typographical quality. Our experimental results show a 4x reduction in compilation time compared to standard pipelines.

content.text: The Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL) requires strict adherence to two-column formatting guidelines. Historically, authors have used LaTeX, which, while powerful, is notoriously difficult to parse programmatically [@Smith2023].

To address this, we introduce a JSON-driven typesetting pipeline. By treating the document as an abstract syntax tree (AST) serialized in JSON, we can trivially validate structural constraints before generating the final Typst markup.
Our system accepts a predefined JSON schema. Nodes in the JSON represent semantic document blocks—such as paragraphs, equations, figures, and tables—which are then recursively compiled.
Typst handles mathematical notation natively. We define our primary loss function $L$ in the following equation:

content.math: L(theta) = - 1/N sum_(i=1)^N log P(y_i | x_i; theta) + lambda ||theta||^2

content.text: The data flows from a raw text generator, into the JSON formatter, and finally to the Typst compiler as illustrated below.
We compared our JSON-to-Typst approach against a traditional scripted JSON-to-LaTeX pipeline. We measured both compilation speed and formatting accuracy across 500 automatically generated papers.

content.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison of compilation methods.

content.text: While our JSON schema handles the vast majority of standard NLP paper structures, it currently lacks support for highly complex, spanning multi-page tables and custom inline graphics (such as TikZ equivalents). Authors requiring deep, pixel-perfect manual overrides may find the strict schema restrictive.

We have presented a robust, JSON-first approach to typesetting ACL papers. By separating the content's structural representation from its visual rendering, we enable faster, safer, and highly automated document generation [@Doe2024].

references.citation: Alice Doe. 2024. The Art of Typst: Next Generation Typesetting. In Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics, pages 102-110, Bangkok, Thailand.
Bob Smith. 2023. Automating Document Generation for NLP. Journal of Code and Linguistics, 15(4):45-59.
